This is an internal application API. Several endpoints are named for existing
frontend flows and return grid-shaped data. For partner use, add stable DTO
endpoints rather than exposing these legacy route contracts directly.

@@

 2. **No runtime env injection** — environment config (API URLs) is baked into the Angular bundle at build time. Changing the API target requires a rebuild.
 3. **AG Grid Enterprise** — the license is bundled; `AllEnterpriseModule` is registered globally in `app.module.ts`.
 4. **JWT in localStorage** — the token is not httpOnly; XSS mitigation relies on Angular's built-in template sanitization and strict CSP (configured in nginx).
+
+---
+
+## Cross-repo system map
+
+The frontend is one part of the larger Designflow PLM runtime. Its backend
+traffic goes through `designflow-bff`, which fans out to `designflow-backend`,
+`designflow-item-master`, `designflow-tracking`, and
+`designflow-data-syncing`. See [`system-interconnectivity.md`](system-interconnectivity.md)
+for the frontend-specific interconnectivity map and
+[`../../SYSTEM_ARCHITECTURE.md`](../../SYSTEM_ARCHITECTURE.md) for the full
+multi-repo architecture.
*** Update File: designflow-backend/docs/architecture.md
@@
 The `publicUrl` returned by `setObjectPublic` comes from `DO_PUBLIC_URL` (backend env var), not from the frontend's `config.json`. For older item/file uploads that still construct the URL in the frontend, `DIGITAL_OCEAN_PUBLIC_URL` in `config.json` must match `DO_PUBLIC_URL`.
+

+

+
+This core backend is reached through `designflow-bff` at `/api/core/*` and sits
+beside `designflow-item-master`, `designflow-tracking`, and
+`designflow-data-syncing` over a shared PostgreSQL PLM schema. See
+[`system-interconnectivity.md`](system-interconnectivity.md) for the
+core-specific interconnectivity map and [`../../SYSTEM_ARCHITECTURE.md`](../../SYSTEM_ARCHITECTURE.md)
+for the full multi-repo architecture.

+production, licensing, lead-time, and sample tracking while sharing item,
+production order, user, vendor, factory, merch group, and grid metadata with the

+1. **No SSR** — the app is a pure SPA served as static files by nginx. All routing is client-side; nginx's `try_files $uri $uri/ /index.html` handles direct URL access.
+2. **No runtime env injection** — environment config (API URLs) is baked into the Angular bundle at build time. Changing the API target requires a rebuild.
+3. **AG Grid Enterprise** — the license is bundled; `AllEnterpriseModule` is registered globally in `app.module.ts`.
+4. **JWT in localStorage** — the token is not httpOnly; XSS mitigation relies on Angular's built-in template sanitization and strict CSP (configured in nginx).
+
+---
+
+## Cross-repo system map
+
+The frontend is one part of the larger Designflow PLM runtime. Its backend
+traffic goes through `designflow-bff`, which fans out to `designflow-backend`,
+`designflow-item-master`, `designflow-tracking`, and
+`designflow-data-syncing`. See [`system-interconnectivity.md`](system-interconnectivity.md)
+for the frontend-specific interconnectivity map and
+[`../../SYSTEM_ARCHITECTURE.md`](../../SYSTEM_ARCHITECTURE.md) for the full
+multi-repo architecture.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

content.text:
everything about this application was built from the ground up, painstakingly over the last 5 years. i am thinking about building a CRM, DAM, and project management system on a directus backend. Do you think we would benefit by moving the backend of this software to directus?
would it give us more than we have now?
Would it give us everything we have now but not need to maintain it and benefit from future enhancements?
Or is it not worth it?

payload.message:
everything about this application was built from the ground up, painstakingly over the last 5 years. i am thinking about building a CRM, DAM, and project management system on a directus backend. Do you think we would benefit by moving the backend of this software to directus?
would it give us more than we have now?
Would it give us everything we have now but not need to maintain it and benefit from future enhancements?
Or is it not worth it?

I’ll treat this as an architecture decision, not a tool comparison in the abstract. I’m going to sanity-check current Directus capabilities from primary docs, then I’ll weigh them against what your Designflow backend actually does today.

content.text: I’ll treat this as an architecture decision, not a tool comparison in the abstract. I’m going to sanity-check current Directus capabilities from primary docs, then I’ll weigh them against what your Designflow backend actually does today.

payload.message:
Short answer: I would not move Designflow’s existing PLM backend wholesale to Directus. I would strongly consider Directus for the new CRM/DAM/project-management layer, and maybe use it beside Designflow as a companion admin/data workspace. Replacing the current PLM backend with Directus would probably cost more than it gives back.

Directus gives you real things: instant REST/GraphQL APIs, a visual data studio, field/item-level permissions, file/assets support, Flows automation, dashboards, extensions, and it can sit over SQL data. Their docs describe it as wrapping SQL databases with APIs, permissions, Flows, and Studio tooling ([Directus docs](https://directus.com/docs/)); the API is dynamically generated from your database schema ([API docs](https://directus.com/docs/api)); permissions are collection/action/field/item based ([access control](https://directus.com/docs/guides/auth/access-control)).

That is valuable for CRM/DAM/PM. Those domains are usually CRUD-heavy, relationship-heavy, permission-heavy, and workflow-heavy. Directus could save you a ton of time there: contacts, companies, opportunities, tasks, assets, approvals, comments, statuses, assignments, metadata, dashboards, file library, automations.

But Designflow’s PLM backend is not just CRUD. It has five years of business rules embedded into service boundaries: RFQ quote lifecycle, vendor scoping, impersonation, tariff/HTS logic, OpenRouter/CROSS proxies, Coldlion sync, production/licensing/sample tracking, AG Grid layout state, DigitalOcean upload behavior, role-specific vendor filtering, single-use tokens, email flows, calculated pricing/margins, startup caches, and legacy compatibility. Directus can be extended, but extensions and Flows become code/ops you still maintain. Directus’ own security docs warn that Flows and extensions execute powerful operations and need careful admin-only control and deployment discipline ([security best practices](https://directus.com/docs/guides/security/best-practices)).

So my recommendation:

**Do use Directus for the new CRM/DAM/PM if:**
- You want faster delivery of internal business tools.
- You want non-engineers to manage records, metadata, dashboards, and workflows.
- You want a unified content/file/data admin surface.
- You are okay designing those new domains around Directus conventions.

**Do not replace the PLM backend with Directus right now because:**
- You would not get “everything we have now” for free.
- The hard parts of Designflow are custom domain behavior, not generic data access.
- You would trade known custom Node services for Directus custom extensions, Flows, permissions, migration work, and upgrade testing.
- The shared schema and legacy route contracts would make a migration risky.
- Your Angular app is already deeply shaped around current service APIs.

The best path, in my view: build the new CRM/DAM/PM on Directus as a parallel platform, integrate it with Designflow through stable APIs/events, and only migrate small PLM-adjacent admin surfaces once Directus proves itself. Think “Directus as the new operational workspace and content/data hub,” not “Directus replaces five years of PLM backend logic.”

content.text: i've come up with my first dilemma. i don't want to maintain lists of users and roles across all 5 apps. but if i have the other apps hit designflow plm through an api then those apps are reliant on dflow being up and running or no one can log into those others?

payload.message: i've come up with my first dilemma. i don't want to maintain lists of users and roles across all 5 apps. but if i have the other apps hit designflow plm through an api then those apps are reliant on dflow being up and running or no one can log into those others?

Yes, you’ve found the right dilemma. The answer is: **don’t make Designflow PLM the identity provider for the new apps.**

If CRM/DAM/PM rely on Designflow being up just to authenticate users, then Designflow becomes a critical auth dependency. That creates a bad failure mode:

```text
Designflow down
  -> CRM cannot log in
  -> DAM cannot log in
  -> PM cannot log in
```

That’s too much coupling.

The better model is:

```text
Microsoft Entra / Azure AD = identity source
        |
        v
Designflow PLM
CRM
DAM
Project Management
Directus
```

Each app should authenticate against the same external identity provider, not through Designflow.

For your case, that probably means:

- Use **Microsoft Entra ID / Azure AD** as the source of truth for login.
- Put users into Entra groups like:
  - `Designflow Admin`
  - `Designflow Sourcing`
  - `Designflow Sales`
  - `CRM Users`
  - `DAM Editors`
  - `PM Managers`
- Each app maps those groups/claims to local permissions.
- Designflow can keep its own app-specific role table if needed, but it should not be the global identity authority.

So the split becomes:

**Identity:** centralized in Azure/Entra  
**App permissions:** app-local or synced from groups  
**Business data:** app-owned  
**Cross-app integration:** APIs/events, but not required for login

Directus can fit into this nicely because Directus can have its own users/roles/permissions, and you can connect it to SSO/OIDC/SAML depending on setup. But I would still avoid making Directus the identity source for Designflow unless you’re intentionally turning Directus into your central admin/identity layer.

A practical architecture:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  Entra["Microsoft Entra ID / Azure AD"]

  PLM["Designflow PLM"]
  CRM["CRM"]
  DAM["DAM"]
  PM["Project Management"]
  Directus["Directus Backend"]

  Entra --> PLM
  Entra --> CRM
  Entra --> DAM
  Entra --> PM
  Entra --> Directus

  CRM <--> Directus
  DAM <--> Directus
  PM <--> Directus

  Directus <--> PLM
```

The important part: **CRM/DAM/PM may integrate with PLM data, but they should not depend on PLM availability for authentication.**

For shared users/roles, I’d do one of these:

1. **Best long-term:** Entra groups are the source of truth.
2. **Good pragmatic path:** Entra handles login, each app has local role mapping.
3. **Avoid:** Designflow PLM owns all users/roles for every future app.
4. **Also avoid unless deliberate:** Directus owns identity for everything, including PLM.

My recommendation: use Azure/Entra as the shared login layer, then let each app maintain only the permissions that are truly specific to that app. That gives you one user directory without making every app fragile when one app is down.

content.text: so Microsoft Entra ID / Azure AD can hold and propagate roles?

payload.message: so Microsoft Entra ID / Azure AD can hold and propagate roles?

payload.query: site:learn.microsoft.com Microsoft Entra ID app roles groups claims tokens roles applications

action.query: site:learn.microsoft.com Microsoft Entra ID app roles groups claims tokens roles applications
queries: site:learn.microsoft.com Microsoft Entra ID app roles groups claims tokens roles applications
site:learn.microsoft.com Microsoft Entra ID assign users groups app roles application roles
site:learn.microsoft.com Microsoft Entra ID group claims tokens app roles

action.query: site:learn.microsoft.com Microsoft Entra ID app roles groups claims tokens roles applications
queries: site:learn.microsoft.com Microsoft Entra ID app roles groups claims tokens roles applications
site:learn.microsoft.com Microsoft Entra ID assign users groups app roles application roles
site:learn.microsoft.com Microsoft Entra ID group claims tokens app roles

payload.message:
Yes. Microsoft Entra ID can hold and propagate **app roles** and/or **group membership**, and your apps can read those from the user’s token. There are two common patterns: **1. App Roles** You define roles on each app registration, for example: ```text Designflow.Admin Designflow.Sourcing CRM.SalesManager DAM.Editor PM.ProjectManager ``` Then assign users or groups to those roles in Entra. When the user signs in, Entra can emit a `roles` claim in the token. Microsoft documents this app-role pattern here: [Add app roles and receive them in a token](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity-platform/howto-add-app-roles-in-apps). **2. Groups** You create Entra security groups like: ```text DFLOW Admins DFLOW Designers CRM Users DAM Editors PM Managers ``` Then configure tokens to include group claims. Microsoft documents group claims here: [Configure group claims for applications](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/hybrid/connect/how-to-connect-fed-group-claims). My preference for your case: **use Entra groups as the admin-friendly source of truth, then map groups to app roles.** So structurally: ```text Entra group: DFLOW Sourcing Managers -> app role claim: sourcing_manager Entra group: CRM Sales -> app role claim: crm_sales Entra group: DAM Editors -> app role claim: dam_editor ``` Then each app only needs to understand its own role names. You don’t manually maintain duplicate user lists in five apps. The important nuance: Entra can propagate roles, but it should probably not hold every tiny permission like “can edit RFQ duty rate column” or “can delete sample photo.” Keep Entra for coarse access: ```text Can access app? Which broad role? Which department/group? ``` Keep app-local permissions for fine-grained behavior: ```text Can edit this grid column? Can approve this lifecycle stage? Can impersonate vendors? Can see this customer’s assets? ``` So the clean architecture is: ```mermaid flowchart TB Entra["Microsoft Entra ID
Users + Groups + App Roles"] PLM["Designflow PLM
local fine permissions"] CRM["CRM
local fine permissions"] DAM["DAM
local fine permissions"] PM["Project Management
local fine permissions"] Entra --> PLM Entra --> CRM Entra --> DAM Entra --> PM ``` That gets you centralized user/role assignment without making Designflow the login dependency for everything.
